content.text: Purpose of Monthly Reliability Reviews

Monthly reliability reviews are the primary mechanism by which SRE teams maintain visibility into the health trajectory of a service — not its current state (that is what dashboards are for) but its direction over time. A service with 99.9% availability this month that burned 80% of its error budget in the last week is in a worse position than a service with 99.85% availability spread evenly across the month. Only a structured review that looks at trends, not snapshots, surfaces this distinction.

For GenAI services, reliability reviews must cover metrics that conventional SRE reviews do not: token economics (are retry storms amplifying provider costs?), model health (have fine-tuned adapter accuracy drifts crossed a threshold?), safety classifier uptime (is the fail-closed path being exercised correctly?), and DR model sync lag (is the failover region serving the same model version as production?). These are operational metrics that have no equivalent in traditional web service SRE.

attrs.alt: Monthly reliability review structure: 60-minute agenda with SLO health dashboard, GenAI-specific metrics, incident and toil trends, and risk register — showing example data with flags for exhausted error budget, rising P1 trend, and retry-amplified spend

content.text: Review Cadence and Participants

The review runs on a fixed schedule — the last Tuesday of each month, 60 minutes — with mandatory attendance from the SRE lead, engineering manager, and a product representative who can make scope decisions when error budget policy choices arise. Optional participants include the on-call rotation lead and the data science contact for model health questions.

The review is not a status update meeting. It is a decision meeting. Every section of the agenda should produce either a confirmed-green status or a specific action item with an owner and due date. Meetings that end with "we should look into this" without an owner and due date produce no improvement.
Role
Attendance
Responsibility in review
SRE Lead
Required
Present metrics data; own SLO burn rate analysis
Engineering Manager
Required
Make scope and feature-freeze decisions when error budget is low
Product Representative
Required
Confirm reliability vs. feature delivery trade-offs
On-Call Lead
Recommended
Represent incident and toil data from the rotation
Data Science Contact
As-needed
Explain model accuracy drift or safety classifier anomalies
Section 1: Prior Action Item Status

The review opens with a 5-minute checkpoint on action items from the previous month. For each open item: is it on track, slipping, or blocked? Blocked items get a resolution path decided in this meeting, not deferred to offline. Action items from reliability reviews that are not tracked to completion create the false impression that the program is functioning when it is not.
Section 2: SLO Burn Rate and Error Budget

SLO burn rate analysis is the core of the reliability review. Burn rate measures how quickly the error budget is being consumed relative to the rate that would exhaust it exactly at the end of the compliance window (typically 30 days). A burn rate of 1.0 means the budget will be exactly exhausted at month end. A burn rate of 3.0 means it will be exhausted in 10 days.
The review must answer four questions for each SLO in scope:
What is the current burn rate?
If above 2.0, immediate action is warranted.
What percentage of the error budget remains?
Below 20% with more than 10 days in the window = high-urgency.
Is the burn rate stable, rising, or falling?
A rising burn rate is more concerning than a high but stable rate.
What caused the largest error budget burns this month?
Attribute burns to specific incidents or changes.
Section 3: Incident and Toil Review

The incident review covers the 30 days since the last meeting. Key signals: total P1/P2 count vs. prior month, MTTR trend, repeat incidents (the same root cause appearing more than once), and on-call toil hours per engineer. Repeat incidents are the most important signal — they indicate that post-incident action items did not resolve the root cause.

Toil hours above 20% of the on-call rotation's working time indicate that reliability work is crowding out improvement work. The standard SRE target is that toil consumes no more than 50% of SRE time, but GenAI services that require manual provider failover, model reload restarts, or fine-tuned adapter refreshes can easily exceed this without explicit toil reduction investment.
Section 4: GenAI-Specific Metrics

The fourth section covers metrics unique to GenAI services that have no equivalent in standard reliability reviews. These metrics determine whether the service is healthy beyond what availability and latency SLOs capture:
Metric
What it reveals
Flag threshold
Retry-amplified token spend %
Provider retry storms; broken circuit breakers
> 2% of total token spend
Fine-tuned adapter accuracy drift
Model behavior diverging from baseline without retraining
> 0.5% accuracy delta vs. eval baseline
Safety classifier block rate
Classifier calibration drift; fail-open risk if too low
< 1.5% or > 5% relative to historical baseline
DR model version lag
Failover region serving stale model; RTO risk
> 24 hours behind primary region
Embedding index staleness
RAG serving outdated documents; hallucination risk
> configured max staleness for this service
Provider cost per output token
Efficiency regression; output verbosity increase
> 10% above budgeted rate
Section 5: Risk Register Update

The risk register is a living list of known reliability risks that have not yet been resolved. Each entry has an owner, a due date, and a status. The monthly review updates this list: resolved items are closed, new items discovered during the meeting are added, and stale P1/P2 items without progress are escalated to the engineering manager.

The risk register is distinct from the Jira backlog. The backlog tracks tasks. The risk register tracks technical risks to service reliability — things that could cause an incident or SLO violation if not addressed. The register should stay short (under 10 active items); a risk register with 30 open items is a backlog that has been mislabeled.
Meeting Outputs and Follow-Through

A reliability review that ends without documented outputs has consumed 60 minutes and produced nothing durable. The meeting chair publishes a structured summary to the team Confluence page within 24 hours of the meeting. The summary contains:
SLO health summary:
green/yellow/red for each SLO with burn rate and budget remaining.
New action items:
title, owner, due date, and Jira ticket ID for every item assigned during the meeting.
Error budget policy decisions:
if a freeze or restriction was decided, state it explicitly with the scope and duration.
Escalations:
items that were escalated to engineering manager or director, with the expected decision date.
Risk register delta:
items added, closed, or escalated since last month.

The single most important discipline is that action items are never left without a named owner. A reliability review where action items are assigned to "the team" or "to be determined" will reliably produce the same findings at the next monthly review.
Summary

Monthly reliability reviews provide the structured visibility that dashboards alone cannot give: trajectory, not just current state. The 60-minute agenda covers prior action item status, SLO burn rate and error budget policy, incident and toil trends, GenAI-specific metrics, and risk register updates. Each section is designed to produce a decision or a confirmed-green status — not a deferral.

For GenAI services, the review must go beyond standard reliability metrics to cover token economics, model accuracy drift, safety classifier health, and DR model version sync. These metrics have no dashboard equivalent in traditional SRE tooling and must be explicitly scheduled into the review agenda or they will not be looked at until they cause an incident.
